title: The Bigger House Illusion
subtitle: Why upgrading home rarely delivers what we expect it to, and what actually helps
excerpt: A reading about the psychological gap between what a better home promises and what it actually delivers. Covers why the wanting is real, why the house rarely answers the deeper question, and what to do with the gap.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on the psychology of adaptation and hedonic adaptation research.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are caught between wanting to upgrade and feeling guilty about the wanting, or when a recent home upgrade has not delivered the feeling you expected.

content.text: The wanting of a better home is one of the most consistent features of adult life. It appears across income levels, across property sizes, and across cultures. Almost everyone who has ever owned or rented a home has, at some point, spent significant mental energy on what the next home would be like.

The wanting is real and often legitimate. The question this reading addresses is not whether to want a better home, but why the better home, when it arrives, tends to deliver less than expected, and what is actually going on underneath the wanting.

The imagined bigger house is a container for a lot of different needs. It promises space, which is really a promise of calm. It promises order, which is really a promise of control. It promises a particular kind of arrival: the feeling of having made it, of being in the place that signals you have reached a certain point in your life.

None of these are unreasonable things to want. Calm, control, and a sense of arrival are genuine human needs. The issue is that a house can only partially deliver on any of them, and what it cannot deliver is the most important part.

Psychologists call it hedonic adaptation: the process by which people return to a baseline level of satisfaction regardless of positive changes in their circumstances. A new house, like a pay rise or a new relationship, produces an increase in wellbeing that tends to be shorter than expected. The brain adjusts. The new house becomes normal. The next improvement becomes the object of desire.

This is not a character flaw. It is how the brain works. The problem is that the model of happiness built around it, in which the next upgrade is the key to the feeling you are looking for, is a model that always requires one more step.
What actually came in with you

The new house comes with a fresh coat of paint and none of the history. But the person who moves in brings everything: the patterns, the habits, the relationship dynamics, the feelings about money, the sense of whether they deserve the good thing. These do not stay in the old house. They travel.

This is why many people who upgrade find themselves, within six to twelve months, noticing the problems: the neighbour, the commute, the kitchen that turns out to be slightly too small, the garden that requires more work than anticipated. The problems are different, but they are a similar weight.

Underneath most house-wanting are a set of needs that a house can only partially meet. The need for calm is often really about workload or relationship stress that the bigger kitchen will not solve. The need for arrival is often about a gap between internal identity and external circumstances. The need for a fresh start is real, but the fresh start is internal.

Identifying the need underneath the wanting is not the same as dismissing the wanting. A better home may be a good and legitimate goal. But if the gap being felt is about something other than the house, buying the house will not close the gap. Knowing this in advance is useful.

The home you have is the one you are living in now. The relationship with it, while working toward something else if that is the goal, is a relationship worth attending to. The habit of living in a home as though it is a waiting room for the next home tends to produce a permanent deferral of the experience of being at home anywhere.
None of this argues against wanting better. It argues for knowing what the wanting is really about, and for not suspending the experience of being at home until the better thing arrives.
Original to homemade.education. It draws on research into how people adjust to change.
